question.text: What is the significance of the year 2008 for currency derivatives in India?

answer.text: Exchange-traded currency futures were launched on Indian exchanges (NSE) for the first time, starting with USDINR futures
India adopted a free-floating exchange rate regime
RBI stopped intervening in the forex market
All OTC currency derivatives were banned in India

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Currency futures on USDINR were launched on NSE on August 29, 2008. This was a milestone — it brought exchange-traded, CCP-guaranteed currency derivatives to India for the first time. Later, EURINR, GBPINR, and JPYINR pairs were added along with currency options. **The Trap:** India maintains a managed float (not free float). RBI continues to intervene. OTC forex derivatives remain active and dominant in volume. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I covers the launch and evolution of exchange-traded currency derivatives in India. Compare with equity derivatives launch (2000) and commodity derivatives. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** 2008 was pivotal — the global financial crisis highlighted counterparty risk in OTC markets, making the case for exchange-traded currency derivatives even stronger.
